title: The University of Texas at San Antonio - Full Episode | The College Tour
body: I’m so confused I did online orientation and so much was left out, no “peer mentor”, no info abt how to receive my UTSA ID, no info abt orgs, no clear tour of campus and how I would find out where my classes were, no help what so ever. I called and called for help got transferred over n over, wasn’t informed properly at all, wasn’t told abt UTSA IDs or given info abt buying parking passes bc I was told they were included in tuition (they aren’t n there r like a million to pick from) so I went in person. Honestly doing this whole process online sucked and rlly discouraged me abt this school. The best way is to go in person and ask for help, I would not have known anything otherwise and they are much more helpful. I went in person for a tour a got one that same day but online the tours were weeks out and wouldn’t let u sign up for one unless u were signed up for UTSA the following year (so if u planned to enter in 2023 n wanted a tour in 2023 there was no option for u and you would have to lie abt your date of enrollment so 2024+)

title: Day in the Life of a Cybersecurity Student
body:
Hey, Grant.
I'm still in high school, but your video has led me to take decisive action. 4-5 months ago, I started changing my daily routine. I was getting up at 5am, I realized that the day is much longer than it looks. Despite the 8 hour school day. I'm getting better every year, but now I also have some issues with myself, so here I am again, watching this video. It was very difficult for me in this kind of mode, but I could feel the results of my work, even though I was in a constant state of stress. This video, gives me hope that I can also. I have university entrance exams in 10 months, now I have to work twice as hard and keep up with everything. I am not ready to leave my dream of doing offensive security and IoT security. Thanks for your hard work, I will try to become a decent professional.

title: A Day in the Life of Cyber Security | SOC Analyst | Penetration Tester | Cyber Security Training
body: I wish people would stop telling kids and hopefuls they don’t need a degree or certifications. I am a tech manager and I can assure you, you do need these things. Not because we think having either makes you better than the person who doesn’t.. because it’s required by HR. Your resume isn’t being picked by us. It’s being fed through a scanner, sent to HR, and then HR forwards what they perceive to be good candidates over to us. YOU DO NEED THESE THINGS OR YOU WILL NOT EVEN MAKE IT PAST THE SCANNER. Can you get lucky and pull it off without both in some small IT atmosphere? Sure. But is that likely? Absolutely not. Just get the degree and certs. Don’t significantly lower your chances of even making the first cut through that HR gateway.
author: @starry.starhwa

author: @CtRAlTtAb

title: AFROTC: Day in the Life
body: It's important to note that this is Virginia Tech, a Senior Military College. These are full time cadets that attend all classes in uniform daily, attend daily formations, and do physical training daily. It's far more than conventional ROTC. Senior Military Colleges run the ROTC programs at their respective schools because they're organized under the institution's Corps of Cadets, so training times typically align with what the Corps of Cadets does. For instance at TAMU most of us are up at 5 am for a 5:30 am sharp start time for morning activities. ROTC physical training (morning physical training with your ROTC branch rather than your Corps unit) occurs on Tuesdays and Thursdays in the same time frame. SMCs are somewhat a hybrid between your average ROTC and the service academies. If you're looking for a fully immersive military experience as a cadet to become an officer, while still having a relatively "normal" college experience, Virginia Tech and TAMU are great options. They're both large universities with good sports atmospheres, and a largely civilian student body, but military culture, history, and tradition is very prominent at both institutions
author: @Mason-hv5zh

title: AFROTC: Day in the Life
body:
Explanation of AFROTC (Air Force Reserve Officer Training Corps)

The Air Force Reserve Officer Training Corps (AFROTC) is a college-based program designed to prepare students for leadership roles as officers in the United States Air Force (USAF). The program offers both academic and physical training while allowing students to complete their degree programs at participating universities.

AFROTC is open to students enrolled in participating schools and offers a pathway to a commission as a second lieutenant in the Air Force upon graduation. The program is divided into two main components:

1. The General Military Course (GMC):
This is typically the first two years of the program, where students focus on learning the basics of military life, leadership, and Air Force operations. This phase involves weekly physical training (PT), leadership labs, and classroom instruction on topics like the history of the Air Force, military ethics, and basic officer responsibilities.


2. The Professional Officer Course (POC):
This phase is for upperclassmen who are selected to continue in the program. It involves more advanced training, including leadership development, and preparation for a career as a commissioned officer. Students in the POC take on more responsibilities, such as leading and mentoring the GMC students, and participate in more challenging leadership labs and physical fitness training.



Day in the Life of an AFROTC Member

A typical day for an AFROTC cadet is a balance between college academics, military training, and personal time. Here’s what a day might look like for a cadet:

1. Morning:

Physical Training (PT): Most AFROTC units begin the day with physical training in the early morning. This can involve running, strength training, or other fitness activities to ensure cadets meet the physical fitness standards required by the Air Force.

Class Time: After PT, cadets head to their regular college classes, working toward their degree in areas such as engineering, sciences, or business. Their academic schedule depends on their major, but the demands of both college coursework and AFROTC training require strong time management skills.



2. Afternoon:

AFROTC Class/Lab: On certain days, cadets attend AFROTC-specific classes or leadership labs, where they learn about military history, leadership theory, and Air Force operations. These classes are often led by active-duty officers and senior cadets, with interactive training on leadership and decision-making.

Study Time: In between classes, cadets also need to stay on top of their academic work. They spend time studying for both their college courses and any AFROTC-related exams or evaluations.



3. Evening:

Leadership Lab (Once a Week): Once or twice a week, cadets participate in leadership labs, which are hands-on training sessions that simulate real-world Air Force scenarios. These may involve command exercises, flight training, or training on Air Force-specific protocols and equipment.

Club Meetings or Team Activities: Cadets may also be involved in extracurricular activities related to AFROTC, such as volunteering, attending special seminars, or participating in military or career-oriented clubs.



4. Weekends:

Field Training or Drill: Once a month, cadets might participate in a drill weekend or field training exercises. These sessions simulate real-world Air Force environments and may include tactical training, leadership development, or airfield operations practice. These sessions give cadets a hands-on opportunity to develop their leadership skills in a team-based environment.




In summary, AFROTC is an intensive, structured program that combines academic study, physical fitness, and military training to develop the next generation of Air Force officers. It’s a challenging and rewarding program that prepares cadets to take on leadership roles in the Air Force after graduation.
author: @InfoSphere-Info

title: AFROTC: Day in the Life
body: Our ROTC days: we wake up at 0400H, reveille, army dozen, rifle dozen, road run, 0630H shower, 0700H line up for breakfast 10 pull ups before we can get our food, then in the mess hall will occupy 1/4 of our seat eat within 10 counts or reverse meal. 0730H classroom instruction/physical activity. TAPS at 2200H. Between 2200H-0400H is the hardest when you can not sleep well because you have to "report" to your upperclassmen for "compliance"... its fun i survived but its not for everybody...
author: @Valencio1031

title: Day in the Life of a Bio Major
body: I took a bio class last semester and my professor had to add 15 points (out of 100) to everyone’s test each time we took one. Every time he would talk to all of us like he was disappointed that we didn’t know the scientific name and entire lineage of every animal. He still ended up curving the entire class grade at the end of the semester too 😂
